title: Date and Time Formats in Server Pages
breadcrumbs: Date and Time Formats in Server Pages
text: Date and Time Formats in Server Pages Date and time formats define the syntax used to display and enter dates and times. In Uniface desktop applications, a field is formatted according to its display format as soon as the cursor leaves the field. On the web, this is done when a webgen (in USPS) or websave (in DSPs) statement is executed. For Data and Time fields, the display format is involved in checking the syntax when the cursor leaves the field. Date and Time Formats Data and time data types in the web environment can be entered with or without separators. The following table shows some possible display formats and examples. Time and Datetime checking in the web environment differs in behavior from traditional client/server applications. Table 1. Date and Time Formats for the Web Display format Data entered Interpretation ddmmyy 021097 2 October 1997 ddmmmyy 02oct1997 2 ??? 1997 (month not checked) ddmmyyyy 110197 1 October 1997 ddmmmyyyy 2oct97 2 ??? 1997 (month not checked) dd-mm-yy 21097 2 October 1997 dd-mmm-yy 2Oct97 2 ??? 1997 (month not checked) dd-mmm-yyyy 21097 2 October 1997 ddmmyyyy hhnnss 21097 71002 2 October 1997 07:10:02 ddmmyy hh:nn:ss 21097 7:10:02 2 October 1997 07:10:02 ddmmyy nn-hh-ss 21097 10:07:02 2 October 1997 10:07:02 Datetime fields allow all date formats and all date plus time formats. When you specify a month using alphabetical rather than numeric characters, for example Oct or October, the month is not checked because the allowed month abbreviations are not known in the web environment. The only check made is that the month name contains at least three characters. The century depends on the $CENTURY_BREAK assignment setting and the current century. Missing Input If a Date, Time, or DateTime field is missing input, Uniface substitutes the following values: Table 2. Default Uniface Entries for Missing Input Part Default Day 1 Month Current month Year Current year Hour 0 Minute 0 Second 0 If a digit is omitted, that is, if the total number of digits entered is not even, or the number of digits in a part is not even, a zero is prefixed to the entry. For example, if you enter 11115 this is considered as 011115 , which represents 1 November 1915 (or 2015), not 11 November 1905 (or 2005). Separators The following characters can be defined as separators: ~ ’ ! @ # $ ^ ( ) - _ = + { } [ ] | : ; ’ " < > , . / Copy You can also use the space bar as separators. When you use separators, leading spaces or zeroes can be omitted from each subsection of a value. For example, you can enter 1 January 1997 as 1-1-97 You can mix separators, for example: dd-mmm-yyyy hh:nn:ss However, you cannot include more than one separator between each subsection, as in: dd:-mmm:-yyyy If you use: dd:mmm-yyyy as a format, 10:-yyyy is represented as 10: currentmonth -1997 .
